Meridian · Service composition

Read a codebase's architecture in one screen.

The Service-composition tab scores every module and class on Robert C. Martin's component metrics and draws them as a health map — so you can decide how to build SOLID architecture, not just trace what runs. A six-move tour, on the deliberately-tangled shopfront app. Real frames from the running tool.

subject · shopfront · 110 units · 8 packages · TypeScript + React
1

The whole system as a health map

▸ open Service composition

read

Every module and class is a scorecard, its left rail coloured by health — green on the ideal line, red far off it. Cards are grouped into a frame per package, wired by their dependencies; the gold wires are the ones that cross a package boundary. One screen shows the shape of the architecture — and where it's under strain (the coupling storm converging on utils at right).

2

The main sequence — where each unit sits

▸ the sidebar's Main sequence scatter

read

Plot every unit by abstractness (A) against instability (I). The dashed diagonal is the main sequence — the healthy balance. The corners are the traps: the red zone of pain (concrete and depended-upon — rigid) and the amber zone of uselessness (abstract but unused). Here almost everything hugs the bottom edge (A≈0) — this codebase has barely any abstraction, and that flatness is itself the finding.

3

What to fix first

▸ the Refactor candidates worklist

read

The design smells, ranked worst-first: HUB coupled both ways, PAIN concrete & depended-upon, SPLIT low cohesion, UNUSED abstract & friendless. legacy.ts and result.ts top the list at D 1.0. Click any row to jump straight to that unit — the list drives the canvas.

4

Focus where you are — not the whole dump

▸ click a unit to root the view there

read

Rooted at BaseRepository, the graph shows just what it touches — its own card, full, plus its one-hop neighbours as faded boundary ghosts you click to travel into. Now the card is legible: Ca 9 (nine dependants), A 0.2 (concrete), cohesion 0.25 → PAIN SPLIT, rail deep red at D 0.62. The breadcrumb walks back out.

5

Jump anywhere

▸ ⌘P → a file or package

read

The same quick-open you'd expect from an editor — ⌘P — but here it roots the composition at any module or package ("Root the composition at…"). Type a name, hit enter, and the map re-centres on that file and what it composes. No hunting the canvas.

6

Blast radius before you touch it

▸ select a shared unit

read

Select Logger and its dependency neighbourhood lights while everything unrelated fades — that's the impact set. Change the logger and you touch exactly those. It's the "who leans on this?" answer you want before you edit a shared unit, read straight off the graph.

Takeaway

Six moves turn a tangled repo into an architecture you can reason about — which units are the right size, which are coupled the wrong way, which package boundaries are fiction, and where to start. The scores are derived from the same edges the extractor already produces, so the map can't drift from the code.

map → main sequence → worklist → focus → jump → blast radius
